summary: Jeff Atwood examines the demanding hardware requirements for playing high-definition video on PCs in late 2006. He breaks down the CPU, GPU, and memory requirements, noting that even gaming-class systems struggle with 1080p playback. The post details the upgrade path needed for HD capability, including HDMI-capable graphics cards with HDCP support and expensive Blu-Ray or HD-DVD drives. Atwood also raises concerns about DRM restrictions that may eventually require digital-only output, effectively closing the 'analog hole' that currently allows playback on older equipment.
key_points: 1080p video playback requires extremely powerful CPUs by 2006 standards—faster than typical gaming requirements
GPU decode acceleration exists but is unreliable; fast dual-core CPUs are a simpler solution
HDCP copy protection is creating artificial requirements for certified video cards with HDMI output
The HD format war means consumers face expensive choices between Blu-Ray ($699) and HD-DVD options
Content protection schemes may eventually block analog output entirely, forcing hardware upgrades
money_quotes: The CPU requirement is off the charts, even by modern gaming standards.
Getting GPU decode acceleration working is a crapshoot at best.
Fast dual core CPUs are cheaper and certainly simpler than dealing with the hassle of offloading the decoding to the video card.
Gotta plug that pesky analog hole eventually, I suppose.
Some Blu-ray Discs or HD DVD titles may require a digital output instead of analog.
key_insight: The transition to HD video on PCs reveals how copy protection schemes like HDCP are driving hardware requirements as much as the actual technical demands of high-resolution playback.
